records.ety: [L. accedere to approach, accede; ad + cedere to move, yield: cf. F. accdere. See Cede.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Acceded; p. pr. & vb. n. Acceding.]

defs: To approach; to come forward; -- opposed to recede.
To enter upon an office or dignity; to attain.
quotes: Edward IV., who had acceded to the throne in the year 1461.
If Frederick had acceded to the supreme power.

defs: To become a party by associating one's self with others; to give one's adhesion. Hence, to agree or assent to a proposal or a view; as, he acceded to my request.
quotes: The treaty of Hanover in 1725 . . . to which the Dutch afterwards acceded.
